summary: Shapiro offers practical tablesaw safety advice through his characteristic humor. He divides woodworkers into two categories — 'safety nuts' who use proper guards and pushsticks, and those called 'stubby' (implying they've lost fingers). The post focuses on a commonly overlooked danger: moving a slightly angled tablesaw fence to the opposite side of the blade, which reverses the tilt and causes dangerous kickback. It's a short, punchy safety reminder from his early blogging days about maker culture.
key_points: Woodworkers who ignore safety equipment risk serious injury, humorously categorized as 'stubby'
A tablesaw fence angled slightly away from the blade becomes dangerous when moved to the opposite side, reversing the tilt toward the blade
Kickback — lumber launched back at the operator — is caused by wood binding between blade and fence
Standing to the side of the saw rather than directly behind it is a critical but often forgotten safety measure
money_quotes: There are two kinds of woodworkers. The first kind is often called the safety nut. The second kind is often called stubby.

If you do, it will be leaning TOWARDS the blade, which absolutely guarantees that the piece will bind between the blade and the fence, and inevitably cause both burning of the piece and rapid projectile motion of your lumber towards your belly button, aka kickback.
key_insight: Safety shortcuts in woodworking — and by extension any hands-on craft — have consequences that range from ruined work to serious injury, making attention to seemingly minor details like fence alignment essential.
